attributes.stem:
A 16-year-old girl presents to the emergency department feeling unwell with a fever. Five days previously she had been discharged from the hospital following a laparoscopic appendicectomy. She had presented a week ago with acute abdominal pain and was found to have perforated appendicitis. The patient initially recovered well from the surgery and was discharged on 2nd postoperative day. Over the past three days, she has developed a high-grade fever associated with chills and abdominal pain.

On examination her blood pressure is 120/70 mmHg, pulse 100/min, temperature 38.7°C and respiratory rate 18/min. There is lower abdominal examination tenderness and a boggy sensation on rectal palpation.

Investigations reveal white blood cell count 15 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0), C-reactive protein 90 mg/L (0 to 5 mg/L), and blood cultures are pending. An abdomino-pelvic CT scan is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
The CT scan shows a large fluid-filled collection in the pelvis which contains gas. This is a pelvic abscess. Pelvic abscess formation is a well recognised complication of perforated appendicitis. However, most cases of appendicitis do not lead to abscess formation.

While antibiotics are crucial in managing intra-abdominal infections, using oral antibiotics and outpatient review alone may not be sufficient to address a localised collection with abscess formation. Small abscesses may be considered for antibiotic therapy alone, especially if they are not causing significant symptoms and if the patient is stable. However, in this particular case, with symptoms like fever and abdominal pain and a confirmed abscess on imaging, some form of active intervention is required. Direct drainage is needed to remove the infected fluid.

Given the known diagnosis (perforated appendicitis) and the patient's current status, CT-guided percutaneous drainage might be an appropriate therapeutic choice for a smaller abscess. This less invasive method facilitates the drainage of the abscess, enabling the removal of infected fluid. It is considered particularly suitable when the patient is in stable condition and the abscess is conducive to percutaneous drainage. This approach has been documented as a viable and effective alternative to surgical interventions, offering a less invasive yet highly efficient means of addressing the pelvic abscess. It is contentious as to whether this large abscess in a young woman with a fever and tachycardia and potential instability is the best way forward.

Endoscopic ultrasound-guided drainage is not typically used for pelvic abscesses. It is more commonly employed for drainage of fluid collections within the gastrointestinal tract or adjacent structures. In the case of a pelvic abscess, CT-guided percutaneous drainage is a more suitable approach. If that was not feasible, then EUS-guided pelvic abscess drainage could be attempted.

In the given scenario, laparoscopic drainage and washout is the safest approach. This is a 16-year-old, almost a week down the line from laparoscopic surgery and has a large pelvic abscess and is showing signs of sepsis (tachycardia and pyrexia). Whilst the textbooks may advocate for percutaneous drainage, in the real world this patient would be taken back to the operating theatre and a thorough laparoscopic washout performed. The appendix stump would be examined to ensure it was sealed and a drain left in site. A pelvic sepsis in a young girl has an associated risk for infertility, so a clear washout is essential. A percutaneous drainage entails a longer hospital stay, requirement for ongoing antibiotics and multiple CT scans to monitor progress. Smaller abscesses or different timing can be managed with other approaches as discussed above. Laparoscopic drainage may also be considered when percutaneous drainage is not feasible, when there has been a failed attempt to drainage or when the patient is unstable.

Opting to wait for a week to repeat CT is not an appropriate course of action given the presence of symptoms and an identified abscess. Immediate intervention, such as laparoscopic drainage, is imperative to prevent further progression of the infection and associated complications.
learningPoints: CT-guided percutaneous drainage has emerged as the most appropriate therapeutic choice for small pelvic abscesses in stable patients.
references: Akıncı D, Ergun O, Topel Ç, Çiftçi T, Akhan O. Pelvic abscess drainage: outcome with factors affecting the clinical success. Diagn Interv Radiol.
Moris D, Paulson EK, Pappas TN. Diagnosis and Management of Acute Appendicitis in Adults A Review. JAMA. 2021; 326:2299-311.
